title: The Pied Piper Of Hamelin
content:
A CHILDпїЅS STORY.

Written for, and inscribed to, W. M. the Younger.


I.

В В В В Hamelin townпїЅs in Brunswick,
В В В В By famous Hanover city;
В В В В The river Weser, deep and wide,
В В В В Washes its wall on the southern side;
В В В В A pleasanter spot you never spied;
В В В В But, when begins my ditty,
В В В В Almost five hundred years ago,
В В В В To see the townsfolk suffer so
В В В В From vermin, was a pity.

II.

В В В В Rats!
В В В В They fought the dogs and killed the cats,
В В В В And bit the babies in the cradles,
В В В В And ate the cheeses out of the vats,
В В В В And licked the soup from the cooksпїЅ own ladles,
В В В В Split open the kegs of salted sprats,
В В В В Made nests inside menпїЅs Sunday hats,
В В В В And even spoiled the womenпїЅs chats
В В В В By drowning their speaking
В В В В With shrieking and squeaking
В В В В In fifty different sharps and flats.

III.

В В В В At last the people in a body
В В В В To the Town Hall came flocking:
В В В В пїЅпїЅTis clear,пїЅ cried they, пїЅour MayorпїЅs a noddy;
В В В В пїЅAnd as for our Corporation, shocking.
В В В В пїЅTo think we buy gowns lined with ermine
В В В В пїЅFor dolts that canпїЅt or wonпїЅt determine
В В В В пїЅWhatпїЅs best to rid us of our vermin!
В В В В пїЅYou hope, because youпїЅre old and obese,
В В В В пїЅTo find in the furry civic robe ease?
В В В В пїЅRouse up, sirs! Give your brains a racking
В В В В пїЅTo find the remedy weпїЅre lacking,
В В В В пїЅOr, sure as fate, weпїЅll send you packing!пїЅ
В В В В At this the Mayor and Corporation
В В В В Quaked with a mighty consternation.

IV.

В В В В An hour they sat in council,
В В В В At length the Mayor broke silence:
В В В В пїЅFor a guilder IпїЅd my ermine gown sell,
В В В В пїЅI wish I were a mile hence!
В В В В пїЅItпїЅs easy to bid one rack oneпїЅs brain,
В В В В пїЅIпїЅm sure my poor head aches again,
В В В В пїЅIпїЅve scratched it so, and all in vain.
В В В В пїЅOh for a trap, a trap, a trap!пїЅ
В В В В Just as he said this, what should hap
В В В В At the chamber door but a gentle tap?
В В В В пїЅBless us,пїЅ cried the Mayor, пїЅwhatпїЅs that?пїЅ
В В В В (With the Corporation as he sat,
В В В В Looking little though wondrous fat;
В В В В Nor brighter was his eye, nor moister
В В В В Than a too-long-opened oyster,
В В В В Save when at noon his paunch grew mutinous
В В В В For a plate of turtle green and glutinous)
В В В В пїЅOnly a scraping of shoes on the mat?
В В В В пїЅAnything like the sound of a rat
В В В В пїЅMakes my heart go pit-a-pat!пїЅ

V.

В В В В пїЅCome in!пїЅ the Mayor cried, looking bigger:
В В В В And in did come the strangest figure!
В В В В His queer long coat from heel to head
В В В В Was half of yellow and half of red,
В В В В And he himself was tall and thin,
В В В В With sharp blue eyes, each like a pin,
В В В В And light loose hair, yet swarthy skin,
В В В В No tuft on cheek nor beard on chin,
В В В В But lips where smiles went out and in,
В В В В There was no guessing his kith and kin:
В В В В And nobody could enough admire
В В В В The tall man and his quaint attire.
В В В В Quoth one: пїЅItпїЅs as my great-grandsire,
В В В В пїЅStarting up at the Trump of DoomпїЅs tone,
В В В В пїЅHad walked this way from his painted tombstone!пїЅ

VI.

В В В В He advanced to the council-table
В В В В And, пїЅPlease your honours,пїЅ said he, пїЅIпїЅm able,
В В В В пїЅBy means of a secret charm, to draw
В В В В пїЅAll creatures living beneath the sun,
В В В В пїЅThat creep or swim or fly or run,
В В В В пїЅAfter me so as you never saw!
В В В В пїЅAnd I chiefly use my charm
В В В В пїЅOn creatures that do people harm,
В В В В пїЅThe mole and toad and newt and viper;
В В В В пїЅAnd people call me the Pied Piper.пїЅ
В В В В (And here they noticed round his neck
В В В В A scarf of red and yellow stripe,
В В В В To match with his coat of the self-same cheque;
В В В В And at the scarfпїЅs end hung a pipe;
В В В В And his fingers, they noticed, were ever straying
В В В В As if impatient to be playing
В В В В Upon this pipe, as low it dangled
В В В В Over his vesture so old-fangled.)
В В В В пїЅYet,пїЅ said he, пїЅpoor piper as I am,
В В В В пїЅIn Tartary I freed the Cham,
В В В В пїЅLast June, from his huge swarms of gnats;
В В В В пїЅI eased in Asia the Nizam
В В В В пїЅOf a monstrous brood of vampire-bats:
В В В В пїЅAnd as for what your brain bewilders,
В В В В пїЅIf I can rid your town of rats
В В В В пїЅWill you give me a thousand guilders?пїЅ
В В В В пїЅOne? fifty thousand!пїЅ was the exclamation
В В В В Of the astonished Mayor and Corporation.

VII.

В В В В Into the street the Piper stept,
В В В В Smiling first a little smile,
В В В В As if he knew what magic slept
В В В В In his quiet pipe the while;
В В В В Then, like a musical adept,
В В В В To blow the pipe his lips he wrinkled,
В В В В And green and blue his sharp eyes twinkled,
В В В В Like a candle-flame where salt is sprinkled;
В В В В And ere three shrill notes the pipe uttered,
В В В В You heard as if an army muttered;
В В В В And the muttering grew to a grumbling;
В В В В And the grumbling grew to a mighty rumbling;
В В В В And out of the houses the rats came tumbling.
В В В В Great rats, small rats, lean rats, brawny rats,
В В В В Brown rats, black rats, grey rats, tawny rats,
В В В В Grave old plodders, gay young friskers,
В В В В Fathers, mothers, uncles, cousins,
В В В В Cocking tails and pricking whiskers,
В В В В Families by tens and dozens,
В В В В Brothers, sisters, husbands, wives,
В В В В Followed the Piper for their lives.
В В В В From street to street he piped advancing,
В В В В And step for step they followed dancing,
В В В В Until they came to the river Weser,
В В В В Wherein all plunged and perished!
В В В В Save one who, stout as Julius Csar,
В В В В Swam across and lived to carry
В В В В (As he, the manuscript he cherished)
В В В В To Rat-land home his commentary:
В В В В Which was, пїЅAt the first shrill notes of the pipe,
В В В В пїЅI heard a sound as of scraping tripe,
В В В В пїЅAnd putting apples, wondrous ripe,
В В В В пїЅInto a cider-pressпїЅs gripe:
В В В В пїЅAnd a moving away of pickle-tub-boards,
В В В В пїЅAnd a leaving ajar of conserve-cupboards,
В В В В пїЅAnd a drawing the corks of train-oil-flasks,
В В В В пїЅAnd a breaking the hoops of butter-casks:
В В В В пїЅAnd it seemed as if a voice
В В В В пїЅ(Sweeter far than by harp or by psaltery
В В В В пїЅIs breathed) called out, пїЅOh rats, rejoice!
В В В В пїЅThe world is grown to one vast drysaltery!
В В В В пїЅSo munch on, crunch on, take your nuncheon,
В В В В пїЅBreakfast, supper, dinner, luncheon!пїЅ
В В В В пїЅAnd just as a bulky sugar-puncheon,
В В В В пїЅAll ready staved, like a great sun shone
В В В В пїЅGlorious scarce an inch before me,
В В В В пїЅJust as methought it said, Come, bore me!
В В В В пїЅI found the Weser rolling oпїЅer me.пїЅ

VIII.

В В В В You should have heard the Hamelin people
В В В В Ringing the bells till they rocked the steeple.
В В В В пїЅGo,пїЅ cried the Mayor, пїЅand get long poles,
В В В В пїЅPoke out the nests and block up the holes!
В В В В пїЅConsult with carpenters and builders,
В В В В пїЅAnd leave in our town not even a trace
В В В В пїЅOf the rats!пїЅ when suddenly, up the face
В В В В Of the Piper perked in the market-place,
В В В В With a, пїЅFirst, if you please, my thousand guilders!пїЅ

IX.

В В В В A thousand guilders! The Mayor looked blue;
В В В В So did the Corporation too.
В В В В For council dinners made rare havock
В В В В With Claret, Moselle, Vin-de-Grave, Hock;
В В В В And half the money would replenish
В В В В Their cellarпїЅs biggest butt with Rhenish.
В В В В To pay this sum to a wandering fellow
В В В В With a gipsy coat of red and yellow!
В В В В пїЅBeside,пїЅ quoth the Mayor with a knowing wink,
В В В В пїЅOur business was done at the riverпїЅs brink;
В В В В пїЅWe saw with our eyes the vermin sink,
В В В В пїЅAnd whatпїЅs dead canпїЅt come to life, I think.
В В В В пїЅSo, friend, weпїЅre not the folks to shrink
В В В В пїЅFrom the duty of giving you something for drink,
В В В В пїЅAnd a matter of money to put in your poke;
В В В В пїЅBut as for the guilders, what we spoke
В В В В пїЅOf them, as you very well know, was in joke.
В В В В пїЅBeside, our losses have made us thrifty.
В В В В пїЅA thousand guilders! Come, take fifty!пїЅ

X.

В В В В The PiperпїЅs face fell, and he cried
В В В В пїЅNo trifling! I canпїЅt wait, beside!
В В В В пїЅIпїЅve promised to visit by dinnertime
В В В В пїЅBagdat, and accept the prime
В В В В пїЅOf the Head-CookпїЅs pottage, all heпїЅs rich in,
В В В В пїЅFor having left, in the CaliphпїЅs kitchen,
В В В В пїЅOf a nest of scorpions no survivor,
В В В В пїЅWith him I proved no bargain-driver,
В В В В пїЅWith you, donпїЅt think IпїЅll bate a stiver!
В В В В пїЅAnd folks who put me in a passion
В В В В пїЅMay find me pipe after another fashion.пїЅ

XI.

В В В В пїЅHow?пїЅ cried the Mayor, пїЅdпїЅye think I brook
В В В В пїЅBeing worse treated than a Cook?
В В В В пїЅInsulted by a lazy ribald
В В В В пїЅWith idle pipe and vesture piebald?
В В В В пїЅYou threaten us, fellow? Do your worst,
В В В В пїЅBlow your pipe there till you burst!пїЅ

XII.

В В В В Once more he stept into the street
В В В В And to his lips again
В В В В Laid his long pipe of smooth straight cane;
В В В В And ere he blew three notes (such sweet
В В В В Soft notes as yet musicianпїЅs cunning
В В В В Never gave the enraptured air)
В В В В There was a rustling that seemed like a bustling
В В В В Of merry crowds justling at pitching and hustling,
В В В В Small feet were pattering, wooden shoes clattering,
В В В В Little hands clapping and little tongues chattering,
В В В В And, like fowls in a farm-yard when barley is scattering,
В В В В Out came the children running.
В В В В All the little boys and girls,
В В В В With rosy cheeks and flaxen curls,
В В В В And sparkling eyes and teeth like pearls,
В В В В Tripping and skipping, ran merrily after
В В В В The wonderful music with shouting and laughter.

XIII.

В В В В The Mayor was dumb, and the Council stood
В В В В As if they were changed into blocks of wood,
В В В В Unable to move a step, or cry
В В В В To the children merrily skipping by,
В В В В And could only follow with the eye
В В В В That joyous crowd at the PiperпїЅs back.
В В В В But how the Mayor was on the rack,
В В В В And the wretched CouncilпїЅs bosoms beat,
В В В В As the Piper turned from the High Street
В В В В To where the Weser rolled its waters
В В В В Right in the way of their sons and daughters!
В В В В However he turned from South to West,
В В В В And to Koppelberg Hill his steps addressed,
В В В В And after him the children pressed;
В В В В Great was the joy in every breast.
В В В В пїЅHe never can cross that mighty top!
В В В В пїЅHeпїЅs forced to let the piping drop,
В В В В пїЅAnd we shall see our children stop!пїЅ
В В В В When, lo, as they reached the mountain-side,
В В В В A wondrous portal opened wide,
В В В В As if a cavern was suddenly hollowed;
В В В В And the Piper advanced and the children followed,
В В В В And when all were in to the very last,
В В В В The door in the mountain-side shut fast.
В В В В Did I say, all? No! One was lame,
В В В В And could not dance the whole of the way;
В В В В And in after years, if you would blame
В В В В His sadness, he was used to say,
В В В В пїЅItпїЅs dull in our town since my playmates left!
В В В В пїЅI canпїЅt forget that IпїЅm bereft
В В В В пїЅOf all the pleasant sights they see,
В В В В пїЅWhich the Piper also promised me.
В В В В пїЅFor he led us, he said, to a joyous land,
В В В В пїЅJoining the town and just at hand,
В В В В пїЅWhere waters gushed and fruit-trees grew
В В В В пїЅAnd flowers put forth a fairer hue,
В В В В пїЅAnd everything was strange and new;
В В В В пїЅThe sparrows were brighter than peacocks here,
В В В В пїЅAnd their dogs outran our fallow deer,
В В В В пїЅAnd honey-bees had lost their stings,
В В В В пїЅAnd horses were born with eaglesпїЅ wings:
В В В В пїЅAnd just as I became assured
В В В В пїЅMy lame foot would be speedily cured,
В В В В пїЅThe music stopped and I stood still,
В В В В пїЅAnd found myself outside the hill,
В В В В пїЅLeft alone against my will,
В В В В пїЅTo go now limping as before,
В В В В пїЅAnd never hear of that country more!пїЅ

XIV.

В В В В Alas, alas for Hamelin!
В В В В There came into many a burgherпїЅs pate
В В В В A text which says that heavenпїЅs gate
В В В В Opes to the rich at as easy rate
В В В В As the needleпїЅs eye takes a camel in!
В В В В The mayor sent East, West, North and South,
В В В В To offer the Piper, by word of mouth,
В В В В Wherever it was menпїЅs lot to find him,
В В В В Silver and gold to his heartпїЅs content,
В В В В If heпїЅd only return the way he went,
В В В В And bring the children behind him.
В В В В But when they saw пїЅtwas a lost endeavour,
В В В В And Piper and dancers were gone for ever,
В В В В They made a decree that lawyers never
В В В В Should think their records dated duly
В В В В If, after the day of the month and year,
В В В В These words did not as well appear,
В В В В пїЅAnd so long after what happened here
В В В В пїЅOn the Twenty-second of July,
В В В В пїЅThirteen hundred and seventy-six:пїЅ
В В В В And the better in memory to fix
В В В В The place of the childrenпїЅs last retreat,
В В В В They called it, the Pied PiperпїЅs Street
В В В В Where any one playing on pipe or tabor
В В В В Was sure for the future to lose his labour.
В В В В Nor suffered they hostelry or tavern
В В В В To shock with mirth a street so solemn;
В В В В But opposite the place of the cavern
В В В В They wrote the story on a column,
В В В В And on the great church-window painted
В В В В The same, to make the world acquainted
В В В В How their children were stolen away,
В В В В And there it stands to this very day.
В В В В And I must not omit to say
В В В В That in Transylvania thereпїЅs a tribe
В В В В Of alien people who ascribe
В В В В The outlandish ways and dress
В В В В On which their neighbours lay such stress,
В В В В To their fathers and mothers having risen
В В В В Out of some subterraneous prison
В В В В Into which they were trepanned
В В В В Long time ago in a mighty band
В В В В Out of Hamelin town in Brunswick land,
В В В В But how or why, they donпїЅt understand.

XV.

В В В В So, Willy, let me and you be wipers
В В В В Of scores out with all men, especially pipers:
В В В В And, whether they pipe us free from rats or from mice,
В В В В If weпїЅve promised them aught, let us keep our promise.
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
